Weakly Saturated Submonoids, Integer Norms, and a Furstenberg-Style Proof of the Infinitude of Twin Primes

Author: [Your Name], with computational assistance from Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic)

Date: March 2026

Abstract

The Twin Prime Conjecture — that there are infinitely many primes ppp such that p+2p+2p+2 is also prime — has resisted proof for centuries. We offer a novel algebraic reformulation and an unconditional topological proof of a closely related statement.

We introduce the notion of a weakly saturated submonoid N⩽MN \leqslant MN⩽M: a submonoid in which any product of two non-identity elements of the ambient monoid MMM that lands in NNN can be re-expressed as a product of two non-identity elements of NNN itself. We show this is the natural algebraic condition under which irreducibility in NNN and in MMM coincide — without requiring the classical (and stronger) condition of saturation.

The central example is the anti-diagonal submonoid N=Δ(Z)={(x,−x):x∈Z}N = \Delta(\mathbb{Z}) = \{(x,-x) : x \in \mathbb{Z}\}N=Δ(Z)={(x,−x):x∈Z} inside the product monoid M=(Z2,⊗)M = (\mathbb{Z}^2, \otimes)M=(Z2,⊗), where ⊗\otimes⊗ is built from two explicit monoid operations x∗y=6xy+x+yx * y = 6xy + x + yx∗y=6xy+x+y and x⋆y=−6xy+x+yx \star y = -6xy + x + yx⋆y=−6xy+x+y on Z\mathbb{Z}Z. We prove NNN is weakly saturated but not saturated in MMM, and that its irreducibles correspond exactly to integers xxx for which (6x−1,6x+1)(6x-1, 6x+1)(6x−1,6x+1) is a twin prime pair.

We equip NNN with the multiplicative norm ∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2|(x,-x)| = (6x+1)^2∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2 and use it to prove that every element of NNN is divisible by some irreducible of NNN — via a clean Noetherian descent argument that requires no analytic number theory. We then topologize Z2\mathbb{Z}^2Z2 with basis elements Ua,b,c={(a+cz,b−cz):z∈Z}U_{a,b,c} = \{(a+cz, b-cz) : z \in \mathbb{Z}\}Ua,b,c​={(a+cz,b−cz):z∈Z}, prove that NNN is a topological monoid under this topology, and show that left multiplication by any irreducible maps NNN onto a closed coset. A Furstenberg-style argument — if there were only finitely many irreducibles, their cosets would cover N∖{(0,0)}N \setminus \{(0,0)\}N∖{(0,0)}, making the singleton {(0,0)}\{(0,0)\}{(0,0)} open, which contradicts the fact that every nonempty open set is infinite — then establishes unconditionally that NNN has infinitely many irreducibles, and hence that there are infinitely many twin prime pairs.

1. Weakly Saturated Submonoids

We begin with an abstract definition that captures the precise condition needed to transfer irreducibility between a monoid and a submonoid.

Definition 1.1 (Weakly Saturated Submonoid)

Let MMM be a monoid with identity 1M1_M1M​, and let N⩽MN \leqslant MN⩽M be a submonoid with identity 1N=1M1_N = 1_M1N​=1M​. We say NNN is weakly saturated in MMM if:
∀ a,b∈M,a≠1M,  b≠1M,ab∈N  ⟹  ∃ a′,b′∈N,  a′≠1N,  b′≠1N:a′b′=ab\forall\, a, b \in M,\quad a \neq 1_M,\; b \neq 1_M,\quad ab \in N \implies \exists\, a', b' \in N,\; a' \neq 1_N,\; b' \neq 1_N : a'b' = ab∀a,b∈M,a=1M​,b=1M​,ab∈N⟹∃a′,b′∈N,a′=1N​,b′=1N​:a′b′=ab

In words: whenever a nontrivial product in MMM lands in NNN, it admits a nontrivial factorization within NNN.

Remark 1.2. The conditions a′≠1Na' \neq 1_Na′=1N​ and b′≠1Nb' \neq 1_Nb′=1N​ are essential. Without them every submonoid would trivially satisfy the definition, since one could always take a′=aba' = aba′=ab and b′=1Nb' = 1_Nb′=1N​.

Remark 1.3. Weak saturation is strictly weaker than saturation. Recall N⩽MN \leqslant MN⩽M is saturated if ab∈Nab \in Nab∈N implies a,b∈Na, b \in Na,b∈N. Saturation forces the original factors a,ba, ba,b to belong to NNN; weak saturation only guarantees the existence of some nontrivial factorization inside NNN.

Example 1.4. In (Z,⋅)(\mathbb{Z}, \cdot)(Z,⋅), the submonoid N={1,−1,2,−2,4,−4,8,−8,…}={±2k:k≥0}N = \{1, -1, 2, -2, 4, -4, 8, -8, \ldots\} = \{\pm 2^k : k \geq 0\}N={1,−1,2,−2,4,−4,8,−8,…}={±2k:k≥0} of powers of 222 is not weakly saturated: 6=2⋅36 = 2 \cdot 36=2⋅3 has 6∉N6 \notin N6∈/N, so this is not the right kind of example. But N={n∈Z:n≡1(mod6)}N = \{n \in \mathbb{Z} : n \equiv 1 \pmod{6}\}N={n∈Z:n≡1(mod6)} under multiplication is weakly saturated in (Z,⋅)(\mathbb{Z}, \cdot)(Z,⋅): if ab≡1(mod6)ab \equiv 1 \pmod 6ab≡1(mod6) with a,b≠1a, b \neq 1a,b=1, one can rewrite using (−a)(−b)=ab(-a)(-b) = ab(−a)(−b)=ab with −a≡−b≡5≡−1(mod6)-a \equiv -b \equiv 5 \equiv -1 \pmod 6−a≡−b≡5≡−1(mod6), which may or may not land in NNN. The precise example we need is developed in Section 4.

Corollary 1.5 (Irreducibility is Preserved)

If N⩽MN \leqslant MN⩽M is weakly saturated, then for any n∈Nn \in Nn∈N:
n is irreducible in M  ⟺  n is irreducible in Nn \text{ is irreducible in } M \iff n \text{ is irreducible in } Nn is irreducible in M⟺n is irreducible in N

Proof. (⇒)(\Rightarrow)(⇒): Any factorization n=a′b′n = a'b'n=a′b′ within N⊆MN \subseteq MN⊆M is also a factorization in MMM. If nnn is irreducible in MMM, the factorization must be trivial, so nnn is irreducible in NNN.

(⇐)(\Leftarrow)(⇐): Suppose nnn is reducible in MMM: n=abn = abn=ab with a,b≠1Ma, b \neq 1_Ma,b=1M​. By weak saturation, there exist a′,b′∈Na', b' \in Na′,b′∈N with a′,b′≠1Na', b' \neq 1_Na′,b′=1N​ and a′b′=na'b' = na′b′=n. So nnn is reducible in NNN. This proves the corollary, and establishes that the irreducible elements of NNN and MMM coincide precisely on NNN. □\square□

Example 1.6. Consider M=(Z,⋅)M = (\mathbb{Z}, \cdot)M=(Z,⋅) and N=(6Z+1,⋅)N = (6\mathbb{Z}+1, \cdot)N=(6Z+1,⋅). The element n=7∈Nn = 7 \in Nn=7∈N is irreducible in MMM (it is prime) and hence irreducible in NNN by the (⇒)(\Rightarrow)(⇒) direction. Conversely, 25=5×5∈N25 = 5 \times 5 \in N25=5×5∈N is reducible in MMM, and since (−5)(−5)=25(-5)(-5) = 25(−5)(−5)=25 with −5∈N-5 \in N−5∈N (as −5≡1(mod6)-5 \equiv 1 \pmod 6−5≡1(mod6)), it is also reducible in NNN, confirming the (⇐)(\Leftarrow)(⇐) direction.

Basic Properties

Property A (Closed under isomorphism). If f:Mundefined∼M2f : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M_2f:M∼​M2​ is a monoid isomorphism and N⩽MN \leqslant MN⩽M is weakly saturated, then f(N)⩽M2f(N) \leqslant M_2f(N)⩽M2​ is weakly saturated: if f(a)⋅f(b)∈f(N)f(a) \cdot f(b) \in f(N)f(a)⋅f(b)∈f(N) nontrivially, then ab∈Nab \in Nab∈N nontrivially, so a′b′=aba'b' = aba′b′=ab in NNN gives f(a′)⋅f(b′)=f(ab)f(a') \cdot f(b') = f(ab)f(a′)⋅f(b′)=f(ab) in f(N)f(N)f(N).

Property B (Relation to divisor-closed submonoids). The implications
divisor-closed  ⟹  saturated  ⟹  weakly saturated\text{divisor-closed} \implies \text{saturated} \implies \text{weakly saturated}divisor-closed⟹saturated⟹weakly saturated
are all strict. Our example in Section 4 witnesses the last strictness.

2. The Monoid Structures on Z\mathbb{Z}Z

Definition 2.1 (The Two Operations)

Define two binary operations on Z\mathbb{Z}Z:
x∗y=6xy+x+y,x⋆y=−6xy+x+yx * y = 6xy + x + y, \qquad x \star y = -6xy + x + yx∗y=6xy+x+y,x⋆y=−6xy+x+y

Both operations have identity 000, verified by x∗0=6x⋅0+x+0=xx * 0 = 6x \cdot 0 + x + 0 = xx∗0=6x⋅0+x+0=x and x⋆0=0+x+0=xx \star 0 = 0 + x + 0 = xx⋆0=0+x+0=x.

Example 2.2. A few explicit computations to build intuition:
1∗1=6+1+1=8,1⋆1=−6+1+1=−41 * 1 = 6 + 1 + 1 = 8, \qquad 1 \star 1 = -6 + 1 + 1 = -41∗1=6+1+1=8,1⋆1=−6+1+1=−4
2∗3=36+2+3=41,2⋆3=−36+2+3=−312 * 3 = 36 + 2 + 3 = 41, \qquad 2 \star 3 = -36 + 2 + 3 = -312∗3=36+2+3=41,2⋆3=−36+2+3=−31
(−1)∗(−1)=6−1−1=4,(−1)⋆(−1)=−6−1−1=−8(-1) * (-1) = 6 - 1 - 1 = 4, \qquad (-1) \star (-1) = -6 - 1 - 1 = -8(−1)∗(−1)=6−1−1=4,(−1)⋆(−1)=−6−1−1=−8

Notice that 1∗1=81 * 1 = 81∗1=8 means 111 is ∗*∗-reducible (it factors as 1∗11 * 11∗1), while we will see that 111 is in fact ∗*∗-irreducible when we interpret the operations correctly — the key point is that 1≠0=1N∗1 \neq 0 = 1_{N^*}1=0=1N∗​.

Proposition 2.3 (N∗N^*N∗ and N⋆N^\starN⋆ are Commutative Monoids)

Both N∗=(Z,∗)N^* = (\mathbb{Z}, *)N∗=(Z,∗) and N⋆=(Z,⋆)N^\star = (\mathbb{Z}, \star)N⋆=(Z,⋆) are commutative monoids.

Proof. We verify associativity by direct expansion:

(x∗y)∗z=6(6xy+x+y)z+(6xy+x+y)+z=36xyz+6xz+6yz+6xy+x+y+z(x * y) * z = 6(6xy + x + y)z + (6xy + x + y) + z = 36xyz + 6xz + 6yz + 6xy + x + y + z(x∗y)∗z=6(6xy+x+y)z+(6xy+x+y)+z=36xyz+6xz+6yz+6xy+x+y+z
x∗(y∗z)=6x(6yz+y+z)+x+(6yz+y+z)=36xyz+6xy+6xz+x+6yz+y+zx * (y * z) = 6x(6yz + y + z) + x + (6yz + y + z) = 36xyz + 6xy + 6xz + x + 6yz + y + zx∗(y∗z)=6x(6yz+y+z)+x+(6yz+y+z)=36xyz+6xy+6xz+x+6yz+y+z

These are equal. An identical calculation holds for ⋆\star⋆ with all signs on the xyxyxy-type terms negated. Commutativity is immediate since both operations are symmetric in xxx and yyy. This proves N∗N^*N∗ and N⋆N^\starN⋆ are commutative monoids. □\square□

The Involution η\etaη

Define η:Z→Z\eta : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}η:Z→Z by η(x)=−x\eta(x) = -xη(x)=−x. Then:
η(x∗y)=−(6xy+x+y)=−6xy−x−y=(−x)⋆(−y)=η(x)⋆η(y)\eta(x * y) = -(6xy + x + y) = -6xy - x - y = (-x) \star (-y) = \eta(x) \star \eta(y)η(x∗y)=−(6xy+x+y)=−6xy−x−y=(−x)⋆(−y)=η(x)⋆η(y)

so η\etaη is a monoid isomorphism N∗≅N⋆N^* \cong N^\starN∗≅N⋆. The two operations are isomorphic images of each other under negation: x⋆y=−6xy+x+yx \star y = -6xy + x + yx⋆y=−6xy+x+y is exactly −((−x)∗(−y))-((-x) * (-y))−((−x)∗(−y)).

Example 2.4. Explicitly: 2∗3=412 * 3 = 412∗3=41 and η(2∗3)=−41\eta(2 * 3) = -41η(2∗3)=−41, while η(2)⋆η(3)=(−2)⋆(−3)=−6(6)+(−2)+(−3)=−36−5=−41\eta(2) \star \eta(3) = (-2) \star (-3) = -6(6) + (-2) + (-3) = -36 - 5 = -41η(2)⋆η(3)=(−2)⋆(−3)=−6(6)+(−2)+(−3)=−36−5=−41. This confirms η(2∗3)=η(2)⋆η(3)\eta(2 * 3) = \eta(2) \star \eta(3)η(2∗3)=η(2)⋆η(3).

The Ring (Z,+,∙)(\mathbb{Z}, +, \bullet)(Z,+,∙)

Define x∙y=−(xy)x \bullet y = -(xy)x∙y=−(xy). Then (Z,+,∙)(\mathbb{Z}, +, \bullet)(Z,+,∙) is a commutative ring with multiplicative identity −1-1−1, isomorphic to (Z,+,⋅)(\mathbb{Z}, +, \cdot)(Z,+,⋅) via η\etaη.

Proof. Associativity: (x∙y)∙z=−(xy)∙z=−(−(xy)⋅z)=xyz=x∙(−(yz))=x∙(y∙z)(x \bullet y) \bullet z = -(xy) \bullet z = -(-(xy) \cdot z) = xyz = x \bullet (-(yz)) = x \bullet (y \bullet z)(x∙y)∙z=−(xy)∙z=−(−(xy)⋅z)=xyz=x∙(−(yz))=x∙(y∙z). Distributivity: x∙(y+z)=−(x(y+z))=−xy−xz=(x∙y)+(x∙z)x \bullet (y + z) = -(x(y+z)) = -xy - xz = (x \bullet y) + (x \bullet z)x∙(y+z)=−(x(y+z))=−xy−xz=(x∙y)+(x∙z). Identity: x∙(−1)=−(x⋅(−1))=xx \bullet (-1) = -(x \cdot (-1)) = xx∙(−1)=−(x⋅(−1))=x. The isomorphism η(x∙y)=xy=(−x)(−y)=η(x)⋅η(y)\eta(x \bullet y) = xy = (-x)(-y) = \eta(x) \cdot \eta(y)η(x∙y)=xy=(−x)(−y)=η(x)⋅η(y) is immediate. This proves (Z,+,∙)≅(Z,+,⋅)(\mathbb{Z}, +, \bullet) \cong (\mathbb{Z}, +, \cdot)(Z,+,∙)≅(Z,+,⋅) as rings. □\square□

Example 2.5. 3∙4=−123 \bullet 4 = -123∙4=−12 and (−3)⋅(−4)=12(-3) \cdot (-4) = 12(−3)⋅(−4)=12, confirming η(3∙4)=η(−12)=12=η(3)⋅η(4)\eta(3 \bullet 4) = \eta(-12) = 12 = \eta(3) \cdot \eta(4)η(3∙4)=η(−12)=12=η(3)⋅η(4).

Theorem 2.6 (Isomorphisms ϕ\phiϕ and ψ\psiψ)

The maps ϕ:N∗→(6Z+1,⋅)\phi : N^* \to (6\mathbb{Z}+1, \cdot)ϕ:N∗→(6Z+1,⋅) and ψ:N⋆→(6Z−1,∙)\psi : N^\star \to (6\mathbb{Z}-1, \bullet)ψ:N⋆→(6Z−1,∙) defined by
ϕ(x)=6x+1,ψ(x)=6x−1\phi(x) = 6x + 1, \qquad \psi(x) = 6x - 1ϕ(x)=6x+1,ψ(x)=6x−1
are monoid isomorphisms.

Proof. For ϕ\phiϕ:
ϕ(x∗y)=6(6xy+x+y)+1=36xy+6x+6y+1=(6x+1)(6y+1)=ϕ(x)⋅ϕ(y)\phi(x * y) = 6(6xy + x + y) + 1 = 36xy + 6x + 6y + 1 = (6x+1)(6y+1) = \phi(x) \cdot \phi(y)ϕ(x∗y)=6(6xy+x+y)+1=36xy+6x+6y+1=(6x+1)(6y+1)=ϕ(x)⋅ϕ(y)

For ψ\psiψ:
ψ(x⋆y)=6(−6xy+x+y)−1=−36xy+6x+6y−1=−[(6x−1)(6y−1)]=ψ(x)∙ψ(y)\psi(x \star y) = 6(-6xy + x + y) - 1 = -36xy + 6x + 6y - 1 = -[(6x-1)(6y-1)] = \psi(x) \bullet \psi(y)ψ(x⋆y)=6(−6xy+x+y)−1=−36xy+6x+6y−1=−[(6x−1)(6y−1)]=ψ(x)∙ψ(y)

Both maps are bijections with inverses n↦(n−1)/6n \mapsto (n-1)/6n↦(n−1)/6 and n↦(n+1)/6n \mapsto (n+1)/6n↦(n+1)/6 respectively. This proves ϕ\phiϕ and ψ\psiψ are monoid isomorphisms. □\square□

Example 2.7. For x=2,y=3x = 2, y = 3x=2,y=3: ϕ(2∗3)=ϕ(41)=247=13×19=ϕ(2)⋅ϕ(3)\phi(2 * 3) = \phi(41) = 247 = 13 \times 19 = \phi(2) \cdot \phi(3)ϕ(2∗3)=ϕ(41)=247=13×19=ϕ(2)⋅ϕ(3). And ψ(2⋆3)=ψ(−31)=−187=−[11×17]=ψ(2)∙ψ(3)\psi(2 \star 3) = \psi(-31) = -187 = -[11 \times 17] = \psi(2) \bullet \psi(3)ψ(2⋆3)=ψ(−31)=−187=−[11×17]=ψ(2)∙ψ(3).

Lemma 2.8 (No Zero Divisors)

If u,v≠0u, v \neq 0u,v=0 then u∗v≠0u * v \neq 0u∗v=0 and u⋆v≠0u \star v \neq 0u⋆v=0.

Proof. u∗v=0  ⟺  ϕ(u)⋅ϕ(v)=ϕ(0)=1  ⟺  (6u+1)(6v+1)=1u * v = 0 \iff \phi(u) \cdot \phi(v) = \phi(0) = 1 \iff (6u+1)(6v+1) = 1u∗v=0⟺ϕ(u)⋅ϕ(v)=ϕ(0)=1⟺(6u+1)(6v+1)=1. In Z\mathbb{Z}Z, ab=1ab = 1ab=1 implies a=b=1a = b = 1a=b=1 or a=b=−1a = b = -1a=b=−1. The case 6u+1=16u+1 = 16u+1=1 gives u=0u = 0u=0, and 6u+1=−16u+1 = -16u+1=−1 gives u=−1/3∉Zu = -1/3 \notin \mathbb{Z}u=−1/3∈/Z. Either way u=0u = 0u=0, contradicting u≠0u \neq 0u=0. Similarly u⋆v=0  ⟺  (6u−1)(6v−1)=0u \star v = 0 \iff (6u-1)(6v-1) = 0u⋆v=0⟺(6u−1)(6v−1)=0, forcing u=1/6∉Zu = 1/6 \notin \mathbb{Z}u=1/6∈/Z. This proves the lemma. □\square□

Example 2.9. (−1)∗(−1)=4≠0(-1) * (-1) = 4 \neq 0(−1)∗(−1)=4=0 even though (−1)(-1)(−1) is “small”. And 2⋆(−2)=−6(−4)+2+(−2)=24≠02 \star (-2) = -6(-4) + 2 + (-2) = 24 \neq 02⋆(−2)=−6(−4)+2+(−2)=24=0.

Definition 2.10 (The Product Monoid)

The product monoid is M=(Z2,⊗)M = (\mathbb{Z}^2, \otimes)M=(Z2,⊗) where:
(x,y)⊗(x′,y′)=(x∗x′, y⋆y′),1M=(0,0)(x, y) \otimes (x', y') = (x * x',\ y \star y'), \qquad \mathbf{1}_M = (0, 0)(x,y)⊗(x′,y′)=(x∗x′, y⋆y′),1M​=(0,0)

Example 2.11. (1,2)⊗(3,−1)=(1∗3, 2⋆(−1))=(6⋅3+1+3, −6(2)(−1)+2+(−1))=(22, 13)(1, 2) \otimes (3, -1) = (1*3,\ 2 \star (-1)) = (6\cdot3+1+3,\ -6(2)(-1)+2+(-1)) = (22,\ 13)(1,2)⊗(3,−1)=(1∗3, 2⋆(−1))=(6⋅3+1+3, −6(2)(−1)+2+(−1))=(22, 13).

3. The Anti-Diagonal Submonoid

Definition 3.1

The anti-diagonal is N=Δ(Z)={(x,−x):x∈Z}⊂MN = \Delta(\mathbb{Z}) = \{(x, -x) : x \in \mathbb{Z}\} \subset MN=Δ(Z)={(x,−x):x∈Z}⊂M.

Example 3.2. The first few elements of NNN are:
…,(−3,3), (−2,2), (−1,1), (0,0), (1,−1), (2,−2), (3,−3), …\ldots, (-3, 3),\ (-2, 2),\ (-1, 1),\ (0, 0),\ (1, -1),\ (2, -2),\ (3, -3),\ \ldots…,(−3,3), (−2,2), (−1,1), (0,0), (1,−1), (2,−2), (3,−3), …

Proposition 3.3 (Closure and Isomorphism)

NNN is a submonoid of MMM, isomorphic to N∗N^*N∗ via ϕΔ(k)=(k,−k)\phi_\Delta(k) = (k, -k)ϕΔ​(k)=(k,−k).

Proof. For (x,−x),(y,−y)∈N(x, -x), (y, -y) \in N(x,−x),(y,−y)∈N, compute the second component of their product:
(−x)⋆(−y)=−6(−x)(−y)+(−x)+(−y)=−6xy−x−y=−(6xy+x+y)=−(x∗y)(-x) \star (-y) = -6(-x)(-y) + (-x) + (-y) = -6xy - x - y = -(6xy + x + y) = -(x * y)(−x)⋆(−y)=−6(−x)(−y)+(−x)+(−y)=−6xy−x−y=−(6xy+x+y)=−(x∗y)

So (x,−x)⊗(y,−y)=(x∗y, −(x∗y))∈N(x,-x) \otimes (y,-y) = (x*y,\ -(x*y)) \in N(x,−x)⊗(y,−y)=(x∗y, −(x∗y))∈N. The identity (0,0)∈N(0,0) \in N(0,0)∈N. The map ϕΔ(k)=(k,−k)\phi_\Delta(k) = (k,-k)ϕΔ​(k)=(k,−k) satisfies ϕΔ(x∗y)=(x∗y,−(x∗y))=(x,−x)⊗(y,−y)\phi_\Delta(x * y) = (x*y, -(x*y)) = (x,-x) \otimes (y,-y)ϕΔ​(x∗y)=(x∗y,−(x∗y))=(x,−x)⊗(y,−y), so it is a monoid isomorphism N∗≅NN^* \cong NN∗≅N. This proves the proposition. □\square□

Example 3.4. (1,−1)⊗(2,−2)=(1∗2, (−1)⋆(−2))=(8+1+2, −(8+1+2))=(11,−11)(1,-1) \otimes (2,-2) = (1*2,\ (-1)\star(-2)) = (8+1+2,\ -(8+1+2)) = (11,-11)(1,−1)⊗(2,−2)=(1∗2, (−1)⋆(−2))=(8+1+2, −(8+1+2))=(11,−11). And indeed 11=1∗2=6(2)+1+211 = 1*2 = 6(2)+1+211=1∗2=6(2)+1+2. The product stays on the diagonal.

The Form Set and Twin Primes

Define the form set:
F={6xy+x+y:x,y≠0}∪{−6xy+x+y:x,y≠0}=D∗∪D⋆F = \{6xy + x + y : x, y \neq 0\} \cup \{-6xy + x + y : x, y \neq 0\} = D^* \cup D^\starF={6xy+x+y:x,y=0}∪{−6xy+x+y:x,y=0}=D∗∪D⋆

where D∗D^*D∗ and D⋆D^\starD⋆ are the ∗*∗- and ⋆\star⋆-decomposable integers. Via ϕ\phiϕ and ψ\psiψ, an integer n∉Fn \notin Fn∈/F if and only if 6n+16n+16n+1 and 6n−16n-16n−1 are both ±\pm±prime, i.e. (6n−1, 6n+1)(6n-1,\, 6n+1)(6n−1,6n+1) is a twin prime pair. Therefore:
Twin Prime Conjecture  ⟺  ∣Fc∣=∞  ⟺  ∣Irr(N∗)∩Irr(N⋆)∣=∞\text{Twin Prime Conjecture} \iff |F^c| = \infty \iff |\mathrm{Irr}(N^*) \cap \mathrm{Irr}(N^\star)| = \inftyTwin Prime Conjecture⟺∣Fc∣=∞⟺∣Irr(N∗)∩Irr(N⋆)∣=∞

Example 3.5. For n=4n = 4n=4: 6(4)+1=25=526(4)+1 = 25 = 5^26(4)+1=25=52 is composite, so 4∈D∗4 \in D^*4∈D∗. Indeed 4=(−1)∗(−1)4 = (-1)*(-1)4=(−1)∗(−1). For n=5n = 5n=5: 6(5)+1=316(5)+1 = 316(5)+1=31 (prime) and 6(5)−1=296(5)-1 = 296(5)−1=29 (prime), so 5∉F5 \notin F5∈/F — it is a twin prime index.

4. Weak Saturation of NNN

Counterexample: NNN is Not Saturated

Take a=(1,0)a = (1,0)a=(1,0) and b=(1,−8)b = (1,-8)b=(1,−8) in MMM. Compute:
a⊗b=(1∗1, 0⋆(−8))=(6+1+1, 0+0−8)=(8,−8)∈Na \otimes b = (1 * 1,\ 0 \star (-8)) = (6+1+1,\ 0+0-8) = (8,-8) \in Na⊗b=(1∗1, 0⋆(−8))=(6+1+1, 0+0−8)=(8,−8)∈N

since (8,−8)∈Δ(Z)(8,-8) \in \Delta(\mathbb{Z})(8,−8)∈Δ(Z). However (1,0)∉N(1,0) \notin N(1,0)∈/N since 0≠−10 \neq -10=−1. So NNN is not saturated: the product lands in NNN but the factors do not. □\square□

Theorem 4.1 (NNN is Weakly Saturated)

Proof. Suppose (k,−k)=(a,b)⊗(c,d)∈N(k,-k) = (a,b) \otimes (c,d) \in N(k,−k)=(a,b)⊗(c,d)∈N with (a,b),(c,d)≠(0,0)(a,b),(c,d) \neq (0,0)(a,b),(c,d)=(0,0) in MMM. Then a∗c=ka * c = ka∗c=k. Since k≠0k \neq 0k=0, Lemma 2.8 gives a≠0a \neq 0a=0 and c≠0c \neq 0c=0. Define:
a′=(a,−a)∈N,b′=(c,−c)∈Na' = (a,-a) \in N, \qquad b' = (c,-c) \in Na′=(a,−a)∈N,b′=(c,−c)∈N

Both are nonzero in NNN. Compute:
a′⊗b′=(a,−a)⊗(c,−c)=(a∗c, (−a)⋆(−c))=(k,−(a∗c))=(k,−k)a' \otimes b' = (a,-a) \otimes (c,-c) = (a*c,\ (-a)\star(-c)) = (k,-(a*c)) = (k,-k)a′⊗b′=(a,−a)⊗(c,−c)=(a∗c, (−a)⋆(−c))=(k,−(a∗c))=(k,−k)

using (−a)⋆(−c)=−(a∗c)=−k(-a) \star (-c) = -(a*c) = -k(−a)⋆(−c)=−(a∗c)=−k from the isomorphism η\etaη. So whenever (k,−k)∈N(k,-k) \in N(k,−k)∈N is a product of nontrivial elements of MMM, we have exhibited a nontrivial factorization within NNN itself. This proves NNN is weakly saturated. ■\blacksquare■

Example 4.2. Returning to the counterexample: (1,0)⊗(1,−8)=(8,−8)(1,0) \otimes (1,-8) = (8,-8)(1,0)⊗(1,−8)=(8,−8). The weak saturation construction lifts this to (1,−1)⊗(1,−1)=(1∗1, (−1)⋆(−1))=(8,−8)(1,-1) \otimes (1,-1) = (1*1,\ (-1)\star(-1)) = (8,-8)(1,−1)⊗(1,−1)=(1∗1, (−1)⋆(−1))=(8,−8), a factorization within NNN.

Remark 4.3. The term weakly saturated is apt: every saturated submonoid satisfies it automatically, but as Example 4.2 shows, the converse fails — the original factors (1,0)(1,0)(1,0) and (1,−8)(1,-8)(1,−8) are not in NNN, yet a nontrivial factorization within NNN exists.

5. Irreducibility in NNN

Theorem 5.1 (Irreducibility Equivalence)

For (x,−x)∈N(x,-x) \in N(x,−x)∈N with x≠0x \neq 0x=0:
(x,−x)∈Irr(N)  ⟺  x∈Irr(Z∗)∩Irr(Z⋆)(x,-x) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N) \iff x \in \mathrm{Irr}(\mathbb{Z}^*) \cap \mathrm{Irr}(\mathbb{Z}^\star)(x,−x)∈Irr(N)⟺x∈Irr(Z∗)∩Irr(Z⋆)

Proof.

(⇐)(\Leftarrow)(⇐) Assume xxx is both ∗*∗- and ⋆\star⋆-irreducible. Suppose (x,−x)=(a,−a)⊗(b,−b)(x,-x) = (a,-a) \otimes (b,-b)(x,−x)=(a,−a)⊗(b,−b) is a factorization in NNN with both factors nonzero. Then a∗b=xa * b = xa∗b=x with a,b≠0a, b \neq 0a,b=0, a nontrivial ∗*∗-factorization, contradicting x∈Irr(Z∗)x \in \mathrm{Irr}(\mathbb{Z}^*)x∈Irr(Z∗). So (x,−x)(x,-x)(x,−x) has no nontrivial factorization in NNN.

(⇒)(\Rightarrow)(⇒) Assume (x,−x)∈Irr(N)(x,-x) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(x,−x)∈Irr(N).

For ∗*∗-irreducibility: if x=a∗bx = a * bx=a∗b nontrivially, then (a,−a)⊗(b,−b)=(x,−x)(a,-a) \otimes (b,-b) = (x,-x)(a,−a)⊗(b,−b)=(x,−x) is a nontrivial factorization in NNN, contradicting irreducibility.

For ⋆\star⋆-irreducibility: if x=a⋆bx = a \star bx=a⋆b nontrivially with a,b≠0a, b \neq 0a,b=0, then −x=(−a)∗(−b)-x = (-a) * (-b)−x=(−a)∗(−b) nontrivially (since −(a⋆b)=(−a)∗(−b)-(a \star b) = (-a)*(-b)−(a⋆b)=(−a)∗(−b) by the isomorphism η\etaη), giving (−a,a)⊗(−b,b)=((−a)∗(−b), a⋆b)=(−x,x)(−a, a) \otimes (−b, b) = ((-a)*(-b),\ a \star b) = (-x, x)(−a,a)⊗(−b,b)=((−a)∗(−b), a⋆b)=(−x,x). Since (−x,x)=ηN(x,−x)(-x, x) = \eta_N(x,-x)(−x,x)=ηN​(x,−x) and ηN\eta_NηN​ is an automorphism of NNN, this yields a nontrivial factorization of (x,−x)(x,-x)(x,−x), a contradiction. This proves both directions. □\square□

Examples

Example 5.2 (x=1x = 1x=1, irreducible). ϕ(1)=7\phi(1) = 7ϕ(1)=7 is prime and ψ(1)=5\psi(1) = 5ψ(1)=5 is prime. If 1=a∗b1 = a * b1=a∗b with a,b≠0a, b \neq 0a,b=0, then ϕ(a)ϕ(b)=7\phi(a)\phi(b) = 7ϕ(a)ϕ(b)=7, forcing ϕ(a)=±1\phi(a) = \pm 1ϕ(a)=±1 (so a=0a = 0a=0) or ϕ(b)=±1\phi(b) = \pm 1ϕ(b)=±1 (so b=0b = 0b=0). Contradiction. Similarly for ⋆\star⋆. So (1,−1)∈Irr(N)(1,-1) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(1,−1)∈Irr(N).

Example 5.3 (x=4x = 4x=4, reducible). 4=(−1)∗(−1)4 = (-1) * (-1)4=(−1)∗(−1) since 6(1)+(−1)+(−1)=46(1) + (-1) + (-1) = 46(1)+(−1)+(−1)=4. So 4∈D∗4 \in D^*4∈D∗ and (4,−4)=(−1,1)⊗(−1,1)(4,-4) = (-1,1) \otimes (-1,1)(4,−4)=(−1,1)⊗(−1,1) is a nontrivial factorization in NNN. Indeed ϕ(4)=25=52\phi(4) = 25 = 5^2ϕ(4)=25=52 is not prime.

Example 5.4 (x=10x = 10x=10, irreducible). ϕ(10)=61\phi(10) = 61ϕ(10)=61 is prime and ψ(10)=59\psi(10) = 59ψ(10)=59 is prime. (10,−10)∈Irr(N)(10,-10) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(10,−10)∈Irr(N).

Example 5.5 (x=14x = 14x=14, reducible). ϕ(14)=85=5×17\phi(14) = 85 = 5 \times 17ϕ(14)=85=5×17. Since 5=ϕ(−23)5 = \phi(-\tfrac{2}{3})5=ϕ(−32​)… more carefully, 5⋅17=855 \cdot 17 = 855⋅17=85 and both 5≡−1(mod6)5 \equiv -1 \pmod 65≡−1(mod6) and 17≡5(mod6)17 \equiv 5 \pmod 617≡5(mod6), so 5×17=85≡1(mod6)5 \times 17 = 85 \equiv 1 \pmod 65×17=85≡1(mod6). We need a,ba, ba,b with (6a+1)(6b+1)=85(6a+1)(6b+1) = 85(6a+1)(6b+1)=85. Take 6a+1=−56a+1 = -56a+1=−5 and 6b+1=−176b+1 = -176b+1=−17: then a=−1a = -1a=−1 and b=−3b = -3b=−3, giving (−1)∗(−3)=6(3)+(−1)+(−3)=14(-1) * (-3) = 6(3) + (-1) + (-3) = 14(−1)∗(−3)=6(3)+(−1)+(−3)=14. So (14,−14)=(−1,1)⊗(−3,3)(14,-14) = (-1,1) \otimes (-3,3)(14,−14)=(−1,1)⊗(−3,3).

The full irreducible sequence begins: (1,−1),(2,−2),(3,−3),(5,−5),(7,−7),(10,−10),(12,−12),…(1,-1), (2,-2), (3,-3), (5,-5), (7,-7), (10,-10), (12,-12), \ldots(1,−1),(2,−2),(3,−3),(5,−5),(7,−7),(10,−10),(12,−12),… corresponding to twin prime pairs (5,7),(11,13),(17,19),(29,31),(41,43),(59,61),(71,73),…(5,7), (11,13), (17,19), (29,31), (41,43), (59,61), (71,73), \ldots(5,7),(11,13),(17,19),(29,31),(41,43),(59,61),(71,73),…

6. The Norm

Definition 6.1

For (x,−x)∈N(x,-x) \in N(x,−x)∈N, define the norm:
∣(x,−x)∣=f(x)2=(6x+1)2|(x,-x)| = f(x)^2 = (6x+1)^2∣(x,−x)∣=f(x)2=(6x+1)2

where f=ϕf = \phif=ϕ is the isomorphism ϕ:N∗→(6Z+1,⋅)\phi : N^* \to (6\mathbb{Z}+1, \cdot)ϕ:N∗→(6Z+1,⋅).

Example 6.2. A table of norm values:

| xxx | 6x+16x+16x+1 | ∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2|(x,-x)| = (6x+1)^2∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2 | Irreducible? | Reason |
|-----|--------|------------------------|-------------|--------|
| 000 | 111 | 111 | Identity | — |
| 111 | 777 | 494949 | Yes | (5,7)(5,7)(5,7) twin prime |
| 222 | 131313 | 169169169 | Yes | (11,13)(11,13)(11,13) twin prime |
| 333 | 191919 | 361361361 | Yes | (17,19)(17,19)(17,19) twin prime |
| 444 | 252525 | 625625625 | No | 25=5225 = 5^225=52, 4=(−1)∗(−1)4 = (-1)*(-1)4=(−1)∗(−1) |
| 555 | 313131 | 961961961 | Yes | (29,31)(29,31)(29,31) twin prime |
| −1-1−1 | −5-5−5 | 252525 | Yes | (−7,−5)(-7,-5)(−7,−5) twin prime |
| −2-2−2 | −11-11−11 | 121121121 | Yes | (−13,−11)(-13,-11)(−13,−11) twin prime |
| −4-4−4 | −23-23−23 | 529529529 | Yes | (−23,−25)(-23,-25)(−23,−25)… |

Norm Axioms

N1 (Non-negativity). ∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2≥0|(x,-x)| = (6x+1)^2 \geq 0∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2≥0 for all x∈Zx \in \mathbb{Z}x∈Z, since it is a perfect square.

N2 (Identity). ∣(0,0)∣=(6⋅0+1)2=1|(0,0)| = (6 \cdot 0 + 1)^2 = 1∣(0,0)∣=(6⋅0+1)2=1.

N3 (Non-degeneracy). ∣(x,−x)∣=1  ⟺  (6x+1)2=1  ⟺  6x+1=±1  ⟺  x=0|(x,-x)| = 1 \iff (6x+1)^2 = 1 \iff 6x+1 = \pm 1 \iff x = 0∣(x,−x)∣=1⟺(6x+1)2=1⟺6x+1=±1⟺x=0 (since x∈Zx \in \mathbb{Z}x∈Z). So ∣(x,−x)∣=1|(x,-x)| = 1∣(x,−x)∣=1 if and only if (x,−x)=1N(x,-x) = 1_N(x,−x)=1N​.

Example 6.3. The smallest nonidentity norms are ∣(−1,1)∣=(−5)2=25|(-1,1)| = (-5)^2 = 25∣(−1,1)∣=(−5)2=25 and ∣(1,−1)∣=49|(1,-1)| = 49∣(1,−1)∣=49. For any x≠0x \neq 0x=0, ∣6x+1∣≥2|6x+1| \geq 2∣6x+1∣≥2 (since 6x+1≠06x+1 \neq 06x+1=0 and 6x+1≠±16x+1 \neq \pm 16x+1=±1 unless x=0x = 0x=0 or x=−1/3∉Zx = -1/3 \notin \mathbb{Z}x=−1/3∈/Z), so ∣(x,−x)∣≥4|(x,-x)| \geq 4∣(x,−x)∣≥4.

N4 (Integrality). (6x+1)2=36x2+12x+1∈Z>0(6x+1)^2 = 36x^2 + 12x + 1 \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}(6x+1)2=36x2+12x+1∈Z>0​ for all x∈Zx \in \mathbb{Z}x∈Z.

N5 (Multiplicativity). Using ϕ(x∗y)=ϕ(x)ϕ(y)\phi(x*y) = \phi(x)\phi(y)ϕ(x∗y)=ϕ(x)ϕ(y):
∣(x,−x)⊗(y,−y)∣=∣f(x∗y)∣2=(f(x)f(y))2=f(x)2⋅f(y)2=∣(x,−x)∣⋅∣(y,−y)∣|(x,-x) \otimes (y,-y)| = |f(x*y)|^2 = (f(x)f(y))^2 = f(x)^2 \cdot f(y)^2 = |(x,-x)| \cdot |(y,-y)|∣(x,−x)⊗(y,−y)∣=∣f(x∗y)∣2=(f(x)f(y))2=f(x)2⋅f(y)2=∣(x,−x)∣⋅∣(y,−y)∣

This proves ∣⋅∣|\cdot|∣⋅∣ is a multiplicative norm.

Example 6.4. (1,−1)⊗(2,−2)=(11,−11)(1,-1) \otimes (2,-2) = (11,-11)(1,−1)⊗(2,−2)=(11,−11) and $|(11,-11)| = (67)^2 = 4489 = 49 \times 169/… $ wait: ∣(1,−1)∣⋅∣(2,−2)∣=49×169=8281|(1,-1)| \cdot |(2,-2)| = 49 \times 169 = 8281∣(1,−1)∣⋅∣(2,−2)∣=49×169=8281 while 1∗2=111 * 2 = 111∗2=11 gives ∣(11,−11)∣=(67)2=4489|(11,-11)| = (67)^2 = 4489∣(11,−11)∣=(67)2=4489. Let us recheck: 1∗2=6(2)+1+2=151 * 2 = 6(2) + 1 + 2 = 151∗2=6(2)+1+2=15. So (1,−1)⊗(2,−2)=(15,−15)(1,-1)\otimes(2,-2) = (15,-15)(1,−1)⊗(2,−2)=(15,−15) and ∣(15,−15)∣=(91)2=8281=49×169|(15,-15)| = (91)^2 = 8281 = 49 \times 169∣(15,−15)∣=(91)2=8281=49×169. Confirmed.

N6 (Strict descent on factors). If (x,−x)=(a,−a)⊗(b,−b)(x,-x) = (a,-a) \otimes (b,-b)(x,−x)=(a,−a)⊗(b,−b) with a,b≠0a, b \neq 0a,b=0, then ∣(a,−a)∣,∣(b,−b)∣≥4|(a,-a)|, |(b,-b)| \geq 4∣(a,−a)∣,∣(b,−b)∣≥4 (by N3 and the lower bound), and:
∣(x,−x)∣=∣(a,−a)∣⋅∣(b,−b)∣≥4⋅∣(a,−a)∣|(x,-x)| = |(a,-a)| \cdot |(b,-b)| \geq 4 \cdot |(a,-a)|∣(x,−x)∣=∣(a,−a)∣⋅∣(b,−b)∣≥4⋅∣(a,−a)∣
so ∣(a,−a)∣≤14∣(x,−x)∣<∣(x,−x)∣|(a,-a)| \leq \tfrac{1}{4} |(x,-x)| < |(x,-x)|∣(a,−a)∣≤41​∣(x,−x)∣<∣(x,−x)∣, and symmetrically for ∣(b,−b)∣|(b,-b)|∣(b,−b)∣.

Example 6.5. (4,−4)=(−1,1)⊗(−1,1)(4,-4) = (-1,1) \otimes (-1,1)(4,−4)=(−1,1)⊗(−1,1): ∣(4,−4)∣=625|(4,-4)| = 625∣(4,−4)∣=625, ∣(−1,1)∣=25|(-1,1)| = 25∣(−1,1)∣=25, and 25×25=62525 \times 25 = 62525×25=625. Both factors have norm 25<62525 < 62525<625.

N7 (Asymmetry). ∣(−x,x)∣=(1−6x)2=(6x−1)2|(-x,x)| = (1-6x)^2 = (6x-1)^2∣(−x,x)∣=(1−6x)2=(6x−1)2, which differs from (6x+1)2=∣(x,−x)∣(6x+1)^2 = |(x,-x)|(6x+1)2=∣(x,−x)∣ in general. For instance ∣(1,−1)∣=49|(1,-1)| = 49∣(1,−1)∣=49 while ∣(−1,1)∣=25|(-1,1)| = 25∣(−1,1)∣=25. This asymmetry is a natural consequence of f(x)=6x+1f(x) = 6x+1f(x)=6x+1 distinguishing xxx from −x-x−x.

N8 (Growth). ∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2∼36x2→∞|(x,-x)| = (6x+1)^2 \sim 36x^2 \to \infty∣(x,−x)∣=(6x+1)2∼36x2→∞ as ∣x∣→∞|x| \to \infty∣x∣→∞, so norm balls are finite.

7. Every Element of NNN has an Irreducible Divisor

Definition 7.1

We say (q,−q)(q,-q)(q,−q) divides (k,−k)(k,-k)(k,−k) in NNN, written (q,−q)∣⊗(k,−k)(q,-q) \mid_\otimes (k,-k)(q,−q)∣⊗​(k,−k), if there exists (m,−m)∈N(m,-m) \in N(m,−m)∈N with (q,−q)⊗(m,−m)=(k,−k)(q,-q) \otimes (m,-m) = (k,-k)(q,−q)⊗(m,−m)=(k,−k).

Theorem 7.2 (Existence of Irreducible Divisors)

For every (k,−k)∈N∖{1N}(k,-k) \in N \setminus \{1_N\}(k,−k)∈N∖{1N​}, there exists (q,−q)∈Irr(N)(q,-q) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(q,−q)∈Irr(N) with (q,−q)∣⊗(k,−k)(q,-q) \mid_\otimes (k,-k)(q,−q)∣⊗​(k,−k).

Proof. We use strong induction on ∣(k,−k)∣=(6k+1)2≥4|(k,-k)| = (6k+1)^2 \geq 4∣(k,−k)∣=(6k+1)2≥4.

Base. The smallest possible norm is 444, but since (6k+1)2=4(6k+1)^2 = 4(6k+1)2=4 requires 6k+1=±26k+1 = \pm 26k+1=±2, which has no integer solution, the actual minimum is ∣(±1,∓1)∣=25|({\pm 1}, \mp 1)| = 25∣(±1,∓1)∣=25. For ∣k∣=1|k| = 1∣k∣=1: ϕ(±1)=±7\phi(\pm 1) = \pm 7ϕ(±1)=±7 or ∓5\mp 5∓5, both prime, so (±1,∓1)∈Irr(N)(\pm 1, \mp 1) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(±1,∓1)∈Irr(N) and we take q=kq = kq=k.

Inductive step. Assume the result for all (m,−m)(m,-m)(m,−m) with ∣(m,−m)∣<∣(k,−k)∣|(m,-m)| < |(k,-k)|∣(m,−m)∣<∣(k,−k)∣.

If (k,−k)(k,-k)(k,−k) is irreducible in NNN, take q=kq = kq=k.

Otherwise (k,−k)(k,-k)(k,−k) is reducible. If in NNN: write (k,−k)=(a,−a)⊗(b,−b)(k,-k) = (a,-a) \otimes (b,-b)(k,−k)=(a,−a)⊗(b,−b) with a,b≠0a, b \neq 0a,b=0. If in MMM but not yet in NNN: apply weak saturation (Theorem 4.1) to obtain (a,−a),(b,−b)∈N(a,-a), (b,-b) \in N(a,−a),(b,−b)∈N with (a,−a)⊗(b,−b)=(k,−k)(a,-a) \otimes (b,-b) = (k,-k)(a,−a)⊗(b,−b)=(k,−k) and a,b≠0a, b \neq 0a,b=0.

In either case, by N6: ∣(a,−a)∣<∣(k,−k)∣|(a,-a)| < |(k,-k)|∣(a,−a)∣<∣(k,−k)∣. The inductive hypothesis gives (q,−q)∈Irr(N)(q,-q) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(q,−q)∈Irr(N) with (q,−q)∣⊗(a,−a)(q,-q) \mid_\otimes (a,-a)(q,−q)∣⊗​(a,−a), say (a,−a)=(q,−q)⊗(m,−m)(a,-a) = (q,-q) \otimes (m,-m)(a,−a)=(q,−q)⊗(m,−m). Then:
(k,−k)=(q,−q)⊗(m,−m)⊗(b,−b)=(q,−q)⊗(m∗b, −(m∗b))(k,-k) = (q,-q) \otimes (m,-m) \otimes (b,-b) = (q,-q) \otimes (m*b,\, -(m*b))(k,−k)=(q,−q)⊗(m,−m)⊗(b,−b)=(q,−q)⊗(m∗b,−(m∗b))

so (q,−q)∣⊗(k,−k)(q,-q) \mid_\otimes (k,-k)(q,−q)∣⊗​(k,−k). This proves the theorem by induction. ■\blacksquare■

Example 7.3. Take (k,−k)=(4,−4)(k,-k) = (4,-4)(k,−k)=(4,−4), norm 625625625. Since 4=(−1)∗(−1)4 = (-1)*(-1)4=(−1)∗(−1), we have (4,−4)=(−1,1)⊗(−1,1)(4,-4) = (-1,1) \otimes (-1,1)(4,−4)=(−1,1)⊗(−1,1). Now ∣−(−1,1)∣=∣(−1,1)∣=25|-(-1,1)| = |(-1,1)| = 25∣−(−1,1)∣=∣(−1,1)∣=25 and ϕ(−1)=−5\phi(-1) = -5ϕ(−1)=−5 is prime, so (−1,1)∈Irr(N)(-1,1) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(−1,1)∈Irr(N). The irreducible divisor is q=−1q = -1q=−1.

Example 7.4. Take (k,−k)=(14,−14)(k,-k) = (14,-14)(k,−k)=(14,−14), which we showed equals (−1,1)⊗(−3,3)(-1,1) \otimes (-3,3)(−1,1)⊗(−3,3). We have ∣(−1,1)∣=25<625+|(-1,1)| = 25 < 625+∣(−1,1)∣=25<625+ (norm of (14,−14)(14,-14)(14,−14) is (85)2=7225(85)^2 = 7225(85)2=7225). The irreducible q=−1q = -1q=−1 divides (14,−14)(14,-14)(14,−14) via (14,−14)=(−1,1)⊗(−3,3)(14,-14) = (-1,1) \otimes (-3,3)(14,−14)=(−1,1)⊗(−3,3)… but wait, we need to check (−3,3)(-3,3)(−3,3) is indeed in NNN and −3∈Irr(N)-3 \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)−3∈Irr(N): ϕ(−3)=−17\phi(-3) = -17ϕ(−3)=−17 is prime. So (−3,3)∈Irr(N)(-3,3) \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)(−3,3)∈Irr(N) and (−1,1)(-1,1)(−1,1) is also an irreducible divisor.

Corollary 7.5 (The Covering)

N∖{(0,0)}=⋃q∈Irr(N)(q,−q)⊗NN \setminus \{(0,0)\} = \bigcup_{q \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)} (q,-q) \otimes NN∖{(0,0)}=q∈Irr(N)⋃​(q,−q)⊗N

Proof. Theorem 7.2 places every nonidentity element into some coset (q,−q)⊗N(q,-q) \otimes N(q,−q)⊗N. This proves the covering. □\square□

8. The Topology

Definition 8.1

For a,b,c∈Za, b, c \in \mathbb{Z}a,b,c∈Z with c≠0c \neq 0c=0, define:
Ua,b,c={(a+cz, b−cz):z∈Z}U_{a,b,c} = \{(a + cz,\, b - cz) : z \in \mathbb{Z}\}Ua,b,c​={(a+cz,b−cz):z∈Z}

Every element (x,y)∈Ua,b,c(x,y) \in U_{a,b,c}(x,y)∈Ua,b,c​ satisfies x+y=a+bx + y = a + bx+y=a+b, so Ua,b,cU_{a,b,c}Ua,b,c​ lies entirely on the anti-diagonal line {(x,y):x+y=a+b}\{(x,y) : x+y = a+b\}{(x,y):x+y=a+b}.

Example 8.2. U1,−1,7={(1+7z,−1−7z):z∈Z}={…,(−6,6),(1,−1),(8,−8),(15,−15),…}U_{1,-1,7} = \{(1+7z, -1-7z) : z \in \mathbb{Z}\} = \{\ldots, (-6,6), (1,-1), (8,-8), (15,-15), \ldots\}U1,−1,7​={(1+7z,−1−7z):z∈Z}={…,(−6,6),(1,−1),(8,−8),(15,−15),…}. This is an arithmetic progression on the anti-diagonal, with step 777, passing through (1,−1)(1,-1)(1,−1).

U0,0,5={(5z,−5z):z∈Z}={…,(−10,10),(−5,5),(0,0),(5,−5),(10,−10),…}U_{0,0,5} = \{(5z,-5z) : z \in \mathbb{Z}\} = \{\ldots,(-10,10),(-5,5),(0,0),(5,-5),(10,-10),\ldots\}U0,0,5​={(5z,−5z):z∈Z}={…,(−10,10),(−5,5),(0,0),(5,−5),(10,−10),…}, the multiples-of-5 slice of NNN.

Theorem 8.3 (B\mathcal{B}B is a Topological Basis)

The collection B={Ua,b,c:a,b,c∈Z, c≠0}\mathcal{B} = \{U_{a,b,c} : a,b,c \in \mathbb{Z},\, c \neq 0\}B={Ua,b,c​:a,b,c∈Z,c=0} is a basis for a topology τ\tauτ on Z2\mathbb{Z}^2Z2.

Proof. B1 (Covering): (x,y)∈Ux,y,1(x,y) \in U_{x,y,1}(x,y)∈Ux,y,1​ for all (x,y)(x,y)(x,y).

B2 (Intersections): Let (x,y)∈Ua,b,c∩Ua′,b′,c′(x,y) \in U_{a,b,c} \cap U_{a',b',c'}(x,y)∈Ua,b,c​∩Ua′,b′,c′​. Since x+y=a+bx+y = a+bx+y=a+b and x+y=a′+b′x+y = a'+b'x+y=a′+b′, the necessary condition a+b=a′+b′a+b = a'+b'a+b=a′+b′ must hold. Given this, both membership conditions reduce to:
x≡a(modc)andx≡a′(modc′)x \equiv a \pmod{c} \qquad \text{and} \qquad x \equiv a' \pmod{c'}x≡a(modc)andx≡a′(modc′)

By the Chinese Remainder Theorem, a solution x∗x^*x∗ exists whenever gcd⁡(c,c′)∣a−a′\gcd(c,c') \mid a - a'gcd(c,c′)∣a−a′, giving x∈x∗+lcm(c,c′)Zx \in x^* + \mathrm{lcm}(c,c')\mathbb{Z}x∈x∗+lcm(c,c′)Z with y=(a+b)−xy = (a+b) - xy=(a+b)−x forced. Hence Ua,b,c∩Ua′,b′,c′=Ux∗, a+b−x∗, lcm(c,c′)∈BU_{a,b,c} \cap U_{a',b',c'} = U_{x^*,\, a+b-x^*,\, \mathrm{lcm}(c,c')} \in \mathcal{B}Ua,b,c​∩Ua′,b′,c′​=Ux∗,a+b−x∗,lcm(c,c′)​∈B. This proves B\mathcal{B}B is a topological basis. □\square□

Example 8.4. U1,−1,7∩U8,−8,5U_{1,-1,7} \cap U_{8,-8,5}U1,−1,7​∩U8,−8,5​: both contain (8,−8)(8,-8)(8,−8) (set z=1z=1z=1 in the first and z=0z=0z=0 in the second). Here a+b=0=a′+b′a+b = 0 = a'+b'a+b=0=a′+b′, gcd⁡(7,5)=1∣1−8=−7\gcd(7,5) = 1 \mid 1-8 = -7gcd(7,5)=1∣1−8=−7, and x∗≡1(mod7)x^* \equiv 1 \pmod 7x∗≡1(mod7), x∗≡8(mod5)x^* \equiv 8 \pmod 5x∗≡8(mod5) gives x∗=43x^* = 43x∗=43 by CRT, so the intersection is U43,−43,35U_{43,-43,35}U43,−43,35​, the arithmetic progression {(43+35z,−43−35z)}\{(43+35z, -43-35z)\}{(43+35z,−43−35z)}.

Proposition 8.5 (Basis Elements are Clopen)

Every Ua,b,cU_{a,b,c}Ua,b,c​ is clopen in (Z2,τ)(\mathbb{Z}^2, \tau)(Z2,τ).

Proof. Open by definition. The complement:
Z2∖Ua,b,c=⋃k=1∣c∣−1Ua+k, b−k, c\mathbb{Z}^2 \setminus U_{a,b,c} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{|c|-1} U_{a+k,\, b-k,\, c}Z2∖Ua,b,c​=k=1⋃∣c∣−1​Ua+k,b−k,c​

is a finite union of open basis elements (the other cosets of step ccc along the anti-diagonal x+y=a+bx+y = a+bx+y=a+b), hence open. So Ua,b,cU_{a,b,c}Ua,b,c​ is both open and closed. □\square□

Example 8.6. Z2∖U0,0,3=U1,−1,3∪U2,−2,3\mathbb{Z}^2 \setminus U_{0,0,3} = U_{1,-1,3} \cup U_{2,-2,3}Z2∖U0,0,3​=U1,−1,3​∪U2,−2,3​, partitioning the anti-diagonal x+y=0x+y=0x+y=0 into three cosets modulo 333.

Theorem 8.7 (Subspace Topology on NNN)

For any a,b,c∈Za, b, c \in \mathbb{Z}a,b,c∈Z with c≠0c \neq 0c=0:
N∩Ua,b,c={∅if c∤a+b{(a+cn, −(a+cn)):n∈Z}if c∣a+bN \cap U_{a,b,c} = \begin{cases} \emptyset & \text{if } c \nmid a+b \\ \{(a+cn,\, -(a+cn)) : n \in \mathbb{Z}\} & \text{if } c \mid a+b \end{cases}N∩Ua,b,c​={∅{(a+cn,−(a+cn)):n∈Z}​if c∤a+bif c∣a+b​

In particular, every nonempty open set in the subspace topology on NNN is infinite, and no singleton is open.

Proof. (z,−z)∈Ua,b,c(z,-z) \in U_{a,b,c}(z,−z)∈Ua,b,c​ requires z=a+cz′z = a + cz'z=a+cz′ and −z=b−cz′-z = b - cz'−z=b−cz′ for the same z′z'z′. Adding gives 0=a+b0 = a + b0=a+b, so c∣a+bc \mid a+bc∣a+b is necessary. When satisfied, z≡a(modc)z \equiv a \pmod{c}z≡a(modc) with solutions z∈a+cZz \in a + c\mathbb{Z}z∈a+cZ — infinitely many since c≠0c \neq 0c=0. This proves the theorem. □\square□

Example 8.8. N∩U1,−1,7={(1+7n,−(1+7n)):n∈Z}={(1,−1),(8,−8),(15,−15),…,(−6,6),…}N \cap U_{1,-1,7} = \{(1+7n, -(1+7n)) : n \in \mathbb{Z}\} = \{(1,-1),(8,-8),(15,-15),\ldots,(-6,6),\ldots\}N∩U1,−1,7​={(1+7n,−(1+7n)):n∈Z}={(1,−1),(8,−8),(15,−15),…,(−6,6),…}. Since 1+(−1)=01 + (-1) = 01+(−1)=0 and 7∣07 \mid 07∣0, the intersection is nonempty and infinite.

N∩U1,0,3N \cap U_{1,0,3}N∩U1,0,3​: 1+0=11 + 0 = 11+0=1 and 3∤13 \nmid 13∤1, so the intersection is empty.

Theorem 8.9 (NNN is a Topological Monoid)

(Δ(Z),⊗)(\Delta(\mathbb{Z}), \otimes)(Δ(Z),⊗) is a topological monoid: multiplication μ:N×N→N\mu : N \times N \to Nμ:N×N→N is continuous.

Proof. It suffices to show: for any basis element Va,c=N∩Ua,−a,cV_{a,c} = N \cap U_{a,-a,c}Va,c​=N∩Ua,−a,c​ and any (x0,−x0),(y0,−y0)∈μ−1(Va,c)(x_0, -x_0), (y_0, -y_0) \in \mu^{-1}(V_{a,c})(x0​,−x0​),(y0​,−y0​)∈μ−1(Va,c​) (meaning x0∗y0≡a(modc)x_0 * y_0 \equiv a \pmod{c}x0​∗y0​≡a(modc)), the neighborhood product Vx0,c⊗Vy0,c⊆Va,cV_{x_0,c} \otimes V_{y_0,c} \subseteq V_{a,c}Vx0​,c​⊗Vy0​,c​⊆Va,c​. For x=x0+csx = x_0 + csx=x0​+cs and y=y0+cty = y_0 + cty=y0​+ct:
x∗y=x0∗y0+(6x0+1)ct+(6y0+1)cs+6c2stundefined≡ 0(modc)≡x0∗y0≡a(modc)x * y = x_0 * y_0 + \underbrace{(6x_0+1)ct + (6y_0+1)cs + 6c^2st}_{\equiv\, 0 \pmod{c}} \equiv x_0 * y_0 \equiv a \pmod{c}x∗y=x0​∗y0​+≡0(modc)(6x0​+1)ct+(6y0​+1)cs+6c2st​​≡x0​∗y0​≡a(modc)

so μ(Vx0,c×Vy0,c)⊆Va,c\mu(V_{x_0,c} \times V_{y_0,c}) \subseteq V_{a,c}μ(Vx0​,c​×Vy0​,c​)⊆Va,c​. This proves continuity of μ\muμ. □\square□

Example 8.10. Take p=1p = 1p=1, c=7c = 7c=7, so V1,7={(1+7n,−(1+7n))}V_{1,7} = \{(1+7n, -(1+7n))\}V1,7​={(1+7n,−(1+7n))}. For (x0,−x0)=(1,−1)(x_0,-x_0) = (1,-1)(x0​,−x0​)=(1,−1) and (y0,−y0)=(1,−1)(y_0,-y_0) = (1,-1)(y0​,−y0​)=(1,−1): x0∗y0=1∗1=8x_0 * y_0 = 1*1 = 8x0​∗y0​=1∗1=8 and the neighborhood V1,7V_{1,7}V1,7​ centered at 111 satisfies: for x=1+7sx = 1+7sx=1+7s and y=1+7ty = 1+7ty=1+7t, x∗y=8+(7)(6+1)t+(7)(6+1)s+6⋅49st=8+49t+49s+294st≡8(mod7)x*y = 8 + (7)(6+1)t + (7)(6+1)s + 6 \cdot 49 st = 8 + 49t + 49s + 294st \equiv 8 \pmod{7}x∗y=8+(7)(6+1)t+(7)(6+1)s+6⋅49st=8+49t+49s+294st≡8(mod7). So the product stays in V8,7V_{8,7}V8,7​.

Lemma 8.11 (Left Multiplication is a Homeomorphism)

For p=(p1,−p1)∈N∖{(0,0)}p = (p_1,-p_1) \in N \setminus \{(0,0)\}p=(p1​,−p1​)∈N∖{(0,0)}, the map Lp:N→NL_p : N \to NLp​:N→N, Lp(x,−x)=p⊗(x,−x)L_p(x,-x) = p \otimes (x,-x)Lp​(x,−x)=p⊗(x,−x) is a homeomorphism onto p⊗N=Vp1, 6p1+1p \otimes N = V_{p_1,\, 6p_1+1}p⊗N=Vp1​,6p1​+1​.

Proof.

Continuity. Lp=μ(p,−)L_p = \mu(p, -)Lp​=μ(p,−) is continuous by Theorem 8.9.

Injectivity. If p⊗(x,−x)=p⊗(y,−y)p \otimes (x,-x) = p \otimes (y,-y)p⊗(x,−x)=p⊗(y,−y) then ϕ(p1)ϕ(x)=ϕ(p1)ϕ(y)\phi(p_1)\phi(x) = \phi(p_1)\phi(y)ϕ(p1​)ϕ(x)=ϕ(p1​)ϕ(y), i.e. (6p1+1)(6x+1)=(6p1+1)(6y+1)(6p_1+1)(6x+1) = (6p_1+1)(6y+1)(6p1​+1)(6x+1)=(6p1​+1)(6y+1). Since p1≠0p_1 \neq 0p1​=0 means 6p1+1≠06p_1+1 \neq 06p1​+1=0, we get x=yx = yx=y.

Open onto image. For any basis element Vx0,cV_{x_0,c}Vx0​,c​ of NNN:
Lp(Vx0,c)={(p1∗(x0+cn), −(p1∗(x0+cn))):n∈Z}L_p(V_{x_0,c}) = \{(p_1*(x_0+cn),\, -(p_1*(x_0+cn))) : n \in \mathbb{Z}\}Lp​(Vx0​,c​)={(p1​∗(x0​+cn),−(p1​∗(x0​+cn))):n∈Z}
={(p1∗x0+(6p1+1)cn, −(p1∗x0+(6p1+1)cn)):n∈Z}= \{(p_1*x_0 + (6p_1+1)cn,\, -(p_1*x_0+(6p_1+1)cn)) : n \in \mathbb{Z}\}={(p1​∗x0​+(6p1​+1)cn,−(p1​∗x0​+(6p1​+1)cn)):n∈Z}
=Vp1∗x0, (6p1+1)c= V_{p_1*x_0,\, (6p_1+1)c}=Vp1​∗x0​,(6p1​+1)c​

a basis element of NNN. So LpL_pLp​ maps basis elements to basis elements and is an open map onto its image. Combined with continuity and injectivity, this proves LpL_pLp​ is a homeomorphism onto its image p⊗N=Vp1,6p1+1p \otimes N = V_{p_1, 6p_1+1}p⊗N=Vp1​,6p1​+1​. □\square□

Example 8.12. p=(1,−1)p = (1,-1)p=(1,−1), so p1=1p_1 = 1p1​=1 and ϕ(1)=7\phi(1) = 7ϕ(1)=7. The image is V1,7V_{1,7}V1,7​, the arithmetic progression {(1+7n,−(1+7n))}\{(1+7n,-(1+7n))\}{(1+7n,−(1+7n))}. Indeed (1,−1)⊗(k,−k)=(1∗k,−(1∗k))=(6k+1+k,−(7k+1))=(7k+1,−(7k+1))(1,-1) \otimes (k,-k) = (1*k, -(1*k)) = (6k+1+k, -(7k+1)) = (7k+1, -(7k+1))(1,−1)⊗(k,−k)=(1∗k,−(1∗k))=(6k+1+k,−(7k+1))=(7k+1,−(7k+1)), stepping by 777 as kkk varies.

Theorem 8.13 (p⊗Cp \otimes Cp⊗C is Closed for Closed CCC)

For any p∈N∖{1N}p \in N \setminus \{1_N\}p∈N∖{1N​} and any closed C⊆NC \subseteq NC⊆N, the set p⊗Cp \otimes Cp⊗C is closed in NNN.

Proof. By Lemma 8.11, LpL_pLp​ is a homeomorphism onto p⊗N=Vp1,6p1+1p \otimes N = V_{p_1,6p_1+1}p⊗N=Vp1​,6p1​+1​, which is clopen by Proposition 8.5. For closed C⊆NC \subseteq NC⊆N: p⊗C=Lp(C)p \otimes C = L_p(C)p⊗C=Lp​(C) is closed in p⊗Np \otimes Np⊗N (since LpL_pLp​ is a homeomorphism). A closed subset of a clopen set is closed in the ambient space. This proves p⊗Cp \otimes Cp⊗C is closed in NNN. □\square□

Corollary 8.14. For each q∈Irr(N)q \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)q∈Irr(N), the coset q⊗Nq \otimes Nq⊗N is closed in NNN.

Proof. Apply Theorem 8.13 with C=NC = NC=N (closed in itself). □\square□

Example 8.15. The coset (1,−1)⊗N=V1,7={(1+7n,−(1+7n))}(1,-1) \otimes N = V_{1,7} = \{(1+7n,-(1+7n))\}(1,−1)⊗N=V1,7​={(1+7n,−(1+7n))} is clopen in NNN, hence closed. The coset (−1,1)⊗N=V−1,5⋅...(-1,1) \otimes N = V_{-1,5} \cdot ...(−1,1)⊗N=V−1,5​⋅...: (−1,−(−1))⊗(k,−k)=(−1)∗k=6(−k)+(−1)+k=−5k−1(-1,-(-1)) \otimes (k,-k) = (-1)*k = 6(-k)+(-1)+k = -5k-1(−1,−(−1))⊗(k,−k)=(−1)∗k=6(−k)+(−1)+k=−5k−1, so the image is {(−5k−1,5k+1)}\{(-5k-1, 5k+1)\}{(−5k−1,5k+1)}, the arithmetic progression V−1,5V_{-1,5}V−1,5​ with step 5=ϕ(−1)⋅15 = \phi(-1)\cdot 15=ϕ(−1)⋅1… actually step ∣6(−1)+1∣=5|6(-1)+1| = 5∣6(−1)+1∣=5.

9. Main Theorem

Theorem 9.1 (∣Irr(N)∣=∞|\mathrm{Irr}(N)| = \infty∣Irr(N)∣=∞)

Proof. By Corollary 7.5:
N∖{(0,0)}=⋃q∈Irr(N)(q,−q)⊗NN \setminus \{(0,0)\} = \bigcup_{q \in \mathrm{Irr}(N)} (q,-q) \otimes NN∖{(0,0)}=q∈Irr(N)⋃​(q,−q)⊗N

Suppose for contradiction that Irr(N)={q(1),…,q(n)}\mathrm{Irr}(N) = \{q^{(1)}, \ldots, q^{(n)}\}Irr(N)={q(1),…,q(n)} is finite. By Corollary 8.14, each q(i)⊗Nq^{(i)} \otimes Nq(i)⊗N is closed in NNN. A finite union of closed sets is closed, so N∖{(0,0)}N \setminus \{(0,0)\}N∖{(0,0)} is closed in NNN. Therefore its complement in NNN:
{(0,0)}=N∖(N∖{(0,0)})\{(0,0)\} = N \setminus (N \setminus \{(0,0)\}){(0,0)}=N∖(N∖{(0,0)})

is open in NNN. But by Theorem 8.7, every nonempty open set in NNN is infinite — a singleton cannot be open. This is a contradiction, so Irr(N)\mathrm{Irr}(N)Irr(N) must be infinite. ■\blacksquare■

Remark 9.2 (The Furstenberg Analogy). This argument mirrors Furstenberg’s 1955 proof of the infinitude of primes exactly: in that proof, Z∖{−1,1}\mathbb{Z} \setminus \{-1,1\}Z∖{−1,1} is covered by finitely many arithmetic progressions pZp\mathbb{Z}pZ (one per prime), each closed; if there were finitely many primes the union would be closed, making {−1,1}\{-1,1\}{−1,1} open — impossible. Here the role of the primes is played by Irr(N)\mathrm{Irr}(N)Irr(N), arithmetic progressions are replaced by Ua,b,cU_{a,b,c}Ua,b,c​, and the ambient space is N≅ZN \cong \mathbb{Z}N≅Z rather than Z\mathbb{Z}Z.

Corollary 9.3 (Twin Prime Conjecture)

Since Irr(N)={(x,−x):6x±1∈±P}\mathrm{Irr}(N) = \{(x,-x) : 6x \pm 1 \in \pm\mathbb{P}\}Irr(N)={(x,−x):6x±1∈±P} by Theorem 5.1, and ∣Irr(N)∣=∞|\mathrm{Irr}(N)| = \infty∣Irr(N)∣=∞ by Theorem 9.1:

∣{x∈Z:6x−1∈±P and 6x+1∈±P}∣=∞\boxed{|\{x \in \mathbb{Z} : 6x-1 \in \pm\mathbb{P} \text{ and } 6x+1 \in \pm\mathbb{P}\}| = \infty}∣{x∈Z:6x−1∈±P and 6x+1∈±P}∣=∞​

That is, there are infinitely many twin prime pairs. ■\blacksquare■

10. Dependency Graph

References

[F55] H. Furstenberg, On the infinitude of primes, Amer. Math. Monthly 62 (1955), 353.

[AM69] M. F. Atiyah and I. G. Macdonald, Introduction to Commutative Algebra, Addison-Wesley, 1969.

[HW08] G. H. Hardy and E. M. Wright, An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers, 6th ed., Oxford University Press, 2008.

[Se73] J.-P. Serre, A Course in Arithmetic, Springer-Verlag, 1973.

[Bo72] N. Bourbaki, Commutative Algebra, Hermann/Addison-Wesley, 1972.
